title: ACCEPTABLE VITAL SIGN VALUES
content: Age   Category Weight AVPU/GCS RR SPO2 HR BP
ANY deviation from normal VSS is a concern and should NOT be discounted
Patients with ANY vital signs that are persistently outside of the above ranges must be
Escalated to Ambulance Victoria – Consult clinician in cases where patients refuse
Patients with ANY vital signs that are persistently borderline / close to being outside of the
above ranges must be put through to VVED as a minimum

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: For the purposes of the clinical care protocols, a child is defined as being aged under 16 years
The rational for this relates to the physiological parameters and medication doses of older
children being equal to adults. This principle does NOT relate to emotional care, mental health,
OR legal obligations of caring for a person under the age of 18.

title: DEFINITION
content: Patients meeting ANY red flag criteria MUST be transported to hospital by ambulance.
The clinician MUST be contacted in instances where patients meeting red flag criteria refuse
For patients meeting ANY of the yellow flag criteria the responder MUST contact Ambulance
Victoria, VVED, Clinician, Refcom or the patients GP for further advice.

title: CLINICAL FLAGS, 8
content: Age AVPU/GCS RR SPO2 HR BP

title: RED FLAG CRITERIA – ADULTS & PAEDIATRICS
content: ANY vital sign outside of the Acceptable Vital Sign Values
Including resolved anaphylaxis, possible anaphylaxis or the post-adrenaline patient
Unable to walk (when usually able to walk)
Post-tonsillectomy bleeding (of any amount) of up 14 days post-operation
Patients at risk (E.G. domestic violence, elder abuse, child abuse)

title: RED FLAG CRITERIA – PAEDIATRICS ONLY
content: Second presentation within 48 hours to AV or Dr for related complaint
Febrile  38°c in small infants (I.E. up to 3 months old)

title: YELLOW FLAG CRITERIA – ADULTS & PAEDIATRICS
content: Infection not responding to community based care (e.g. oral antibiotics)
Attend hospital with relevant history when appropriate
Syncope (asymptomatic, normal vital signs, normal ECG)

title: YELLOW FLAG CRITERIA – PAEDIATRICS ONLY
content: Ingestion of a dangerous foreign body- asymptomatic / normal VSS

title: DEFINITION
content: A framework that provides a structured and comprehensive assessment for every patient

title: STOP
content: Dynamic risk assessment / Awareness of personal safety
Rapid assessment / PAT Does the patient appear WELL or UNWELL?
E.G. Major haemorrhage, altered conscious, obvious SOB, pale / sweaty, etc

title: PRIMARY SURVEY
content: -S- Send for help SitRep: Hatzolah Call ambulance + request backup
-IF- Required to direct treatment Perform other specific assessments

title: RESPONDER ACTION
content: -IF- Patient deteriorates at ANY stage Return to Primary Survey

title: ASSESS
content: Use clinical judgement to determine which of the below are appropriate and the order of
-A- Ask About -A- Assessment Tools -A- Assessment Equipment

title: PAUSE & PLAN
content: Call 000 for ALL patients outside of Hatzolah’s clinical scope, requiring hospital care
OR as directed by Hatzolah CPG
-IF- Further clinical advice required Call clinical operations manager via mobile phone
Treatment plan Confirm with patient + team
Support options Consider - E.G. AV, GP, VVED

title: TREAT & REFER
content: -AND- Covered by a Treat & Refer care plan
Reassess: 15 minutely while in Hatzolah care
Ensure patient consents to plan including
-UNLESS- clinical rational provided  VED, NEPT, private Tx (with Clinician consult)

title: DEFINITION
content: The PAT (Paediatric Assessment Triangle) consists of three headings / components...
...which collectively form a rapid first impression as to whether a child is well or unwell

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Visually evaluate level of alertness, muscle tone, body position, chest movement, work of
breathing and skin colour while also looking for obvious injuries
This assessment should not take more than a few seconds
If the child appears well with no signs of serious trauma, approach with a calm demeanour
whilst explaining your actions to the parents and the child
If a well-appearing patient has experienced a high-risk mechanism of injury, consider the
patient potentially unstable due to the risk of serious internal injuries
For children with a poor appearance OR evidence of significant injury, proceed immediately
to the primary survey including any lifesaving interventions as appropriate.

title: DEFINITION
content: Conscious state refers to the level of awareness someone has of their self and their
surroundings. There is a spectrum of consciousness, ranging from being fully alert to
completely unconscious. Hatzolah advises the use of two different conscious state
assessments: AVPU and GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale)

title: AVPU
content: AVPU is quick and simple to apply and is appropriate for determining conscious state whilst
the initial assessment (E.G. Primary Survey) is conducted
AVPU is also the preferred tool for assessing conscious state in children where adapting the
GCS can be problematic. It is widely used and consistent with practice at the Royal Children’s
A child cannot have a conscious state assessment done while asleep. They must be woken first.
If the child wakes and remains awake and alert, record this as an “A” for AVPU. If the child
wakes but remains drowsy and appears inattentive, record this as a “V”.
If a patient has clinical or social issues such as aphasia / dysphasia, facial injuries or language
Barriers then AVPU is an appropriate tool to assess consciousness
AVPU – When assessed is the patient...

title: CONSCIOUS STATUS ASSESSMENTS, 15
content: A formal GCS should be undertaken in more complex patient presentations
The GCS is an objective measure of consciousness. The score should NOT be estimated.
The maximum GCS score is 15 and represents a patient that is fully alert and orientated to
person, place and time. The minimum GCS score is 3 and represents a patient that is
To painful stimuli Reacts to pain 2
Orientated (person, place, time) Appropriate words / smile 5
Localises to pain Localises to pain 5
Withdraws from pain Withdraws to pain 4

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Painful stimuli should be performed in a professional manner as part of a clinical assessment
Painful stimuli should NOT be repeatedly applied to a patient if the expected response is not

title: RECOGNITION
content: -C- Conscious State Alert Alert Alert -OR- Altered -OR-
-S- Speech Clear, Full Sentences Short Sentences Words -OR-
-S- Skin Normal Normal Pale, Sweaty Pale, Sweaty
-W- Work of Normal Slight Marked Marked
Rhythm  Expiratory Phase Expiratory Phase Expiratory Phase
-A- Appearance Calm, Quiet Calm -OR- Distressed -OR- Exhausted -OR-

title:  120 -OR-
content: -S- Sounds Quiet Cough, Mild Cough, Insp/Exp NO Cough, Insp/
Basal Crackles Mid-zone crackles Full field crackles,
Respiratory Rates and Pulse Rates in this table specifically refer to  15 year olds only

title: SIGNS OF REPSIRATORY DISTRESS IN CHILDREN INCLUDE
SIGNS OF HYPOXIA IN CHILDREN INCLUDE
DEFINITION
content: Perfusion refers to the cardiovascular system’s capacity to provide the bodies tissues with
sufficient oxygen and nutrients to meet current requirements to sustain life - while also
removing carbon dioxide and other waste products
The Perfusion Status Assessment involves evaluating four vital signs that collectively give an
insight about a patient's current cardiovascular system function and perfusion status

title: RECOGNITION
content: The patient is considered to have less than adequate ( Adequate) perfusion -IF- 2 or more
of the below criteria are outside of the Adequate criteria

title:  50 -OR-  100
content: No Perfusion Cool, Pale, Clammy No pulse Unable to record Unconscious
Pulse and sBP in this table specifically refer to  15 year olds only

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Always assume abnormal vital signs are due to illness in any patient that appears unwell
An isolate pulse between 50 and 60 BPM may be normal for some adult patients
Particularly if they are very fit (E.G. marathon runner)
An isolate systolic BP below 100 mmHg may be normal for some adult patients
Particularly if they are very small or have paraplegia / quadriplegia

title: WONG-BAKER FACES PAIN RATING SCALE
content: Certain patient groups (E.G. small children) may be unable to understand the 0-10 point pain
Rating system. In such circumstances consider using the Wong Banker FACES Pain Rating Scale.
The Wong Baker FACES Pain Rating Scale was originally created with children for children to
help them communicate their pain to healthcare workers. It is intended for use in patients who
are 3 years or older that are able to understand the tool and choose a face that best illustrates
the physical pain that they are experiencing.
The Wong Baker Faces Pain Rating Scale is NOT intended to be used by a third person to assess
a patients pain on their behalf

title: PAEDIATRIC WEIGHTS
content: For children various treatments are based on body weight, such as drug doses, defibrillation
joules and fluid volume. It is acceptable to ask a parent the patient's weight.
However -IF- weight is unknown it can be estimated using the following calculations:

title: PRINCIPLES OF THE TIME CRITICAL GUIDELINES
content: Any patient meeting ANY Time Critical Criteria MUST be advised hospital transport
Hatzolah crew MUST consult either the Ambulance Victoria Clinician OR Hatzolah Clinical
Operations Manager for patients meeting ANY Time Critical Criteria that are refusing hospital
ANY patient NOT being referred to Ambulance Vicotria OR transported by Hatzolah should be
advised to allow crew to remain on scene and facilitate a review with VVED
A patient meeting any of the following criteria has, or potentially has a clinical problem of
major significance and therefore is Time Critical.
Provide an immediate situation report to dispatch for any patient meeting ANY of the time
With time critical trauma patients, triage and aim for transport to the highest level of trauma
care available within 60 minutes. This may include contacting the AV Clinician.
Medical time critical patients require triage to the nearest appropriate facility.
Scene information may be used in planning for secondary transfer to an appropriate facility as
Patients  16 or  55 years, or those who have a pre-existing medical condition or who are
pregnant may be at greater risk. Using “pay-off”, manage as potentially time critical even if
they don’t fully meet the time critical criteria.
Any Responder who has a “gut feel” that the patient may be Time Critical even though does
Not meet stated parameters should request appropriate and timely support.

title: ACTUAL
content: At the time the VSS were taken the patient was in actual physiological distress
I.E. Altered conscious state, inadequate perfusion or respiratory distress

title: EMERGENT
content: At the time the VSS were taken the patient was not physiologically distressed, but does have a
“pattern of actual injury/illness” which is known to have a high probability of deteriorating to

title: POTENTIAL
content: At the time the VSS were taken the patient was not physiologically distressed, and there was
No significant “pattern of actual injury/illness”, but there is a “mechanism of injury/illness”
known to have the potential to deteriorate to actual physiological distress.

title: ACTUAL TIME CRITICAL - VSS
content: Age SpO2 Respiratory Rate Heart Rate BP GCS
-IF- Trauma Highest trauma service within 60 mins

title: EMERGENT TIME CRITICAL - PATTERN OF INJURY
content: Any penetrating injury (except isolated superficial limb injury)
Serious injury to a single body region such that specialised care or intervention may be
Required or such that life, limb or long-term quality of life may be at risk
Significant injuries involving more than one body region
Limb amputation OR limb threatening injury
Suspected spinal cord injury OR spinal fracture
OR high voltage ( 1000 volts) burn injury
Major compound fracture OR open dislocation
Fracture to two or more of femur / tibia / humerus

title: EMERGENT TIME CRITICAL - PATTERN OF ILLNESS
POTENTIALLY TIME CRITICAL – MECHAISM OF INJURY -&- VULNERABILITY
content: Struck on head by object falling  3 m
-IF- At least 1 mechanism of injury -&- At least 1 vulnerability

title: DEFINITION
content: In the prehospital context safety netting refers to providing patients and caregivers with advice
and instructions for self-care as well providing an adequate contingency plan in case of
unexpected deterioration. This is particularly important when leaving patients at home OR
referring them to further care via non Hatzolah / Ambulance Victoria transport. Insofar as it is
possible this includes patients that refuse management and / or hospital transport despite

title: MANAGEMENT
content: Ensure minimum of 2x VSS covering a span of at least 15 minutes
Discuss results of assessment with patient and the implication of those results
-IF- ANY abnormal findings found Discuss them with patient
Advise limitations of prehospital assessment and the risks associated with such limitations
Discuss the likely course of the illness
-IF- Advised transport -&- Patient refusing Consult clinician
Explain care pathway options including risks and benefits
Provide a recommended course of action if appropriate
Ensure patient agrees and consents with this plan
Their consent MUST be voluntary, informed AND relevant
Share this plan between Hatzolah staff, patient and family / carers (if relevant)
Ensure the plan is implementable (E.G. patient has adequate healthcare literacy)
Confirm patient / carers understand care plan
Explain safety netting / contingency plan in case of deterioration
Confirm patient / carers understand safety netting / contingency plan in case of deterioration
Ask about and address if patient / carer have any further questions or concerns

title: ESCALATION OF CARE
content: -IF- Concerns, further advice required, patient refusing against advice or an Ambulance /
VVED pathway is not being used
Contact the Clinical Operations Manager via mobile phone

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: ANY patients NOT being attended by Ambulance Victoria should at the least have a VVED
Ideally initial VSS should be thorough and include GCS, RR, SpO2, BP, HR, Temperature, BGL
and pupils. Subsequent VSS should include GCS, RR, SpO2, BP and HR. However Temperature,
BGL and pupils should be rechecked if Hatzolah crew deems it relevant.
Consider leaving a copy of the following document with the patient alongside verbal advice
on how and when to access the 3 services

title: SAFETY NETTING, 31
content: Medical Emergency Medical Urgency Medical Uncertainty
Some examples of when to call 000
✱ Sudden numbness or paralysis of the face,
Some examples of when to use VVED
✱ Pain (e.g. abdominal, limb, back or headache)
✱ Heart palpitations or high blood pressure
✱ Mild rash, insect stings or spider bites
You will need a device with a camera to access VVED.
VVED is free, has translator services and is available 24/7.
Wait times are typically less than 30 minutes,
Some examples of when to call Nurse-On-Call
✱ You’re not sure if you should seek medical help
✱ You or someone you’re caring for is feeling unwell
✱ You want general advice on how to care for
✱ You’re far from home or a long way from medical
✱ You want advice or information about health
Nurse-On-Call is free and is available 24/7.
If you need translator services call 131 450
and ask to be transferred to Nurse-On-Call.

title: INEFFECTIVE BREATHING
content: ventilate once every 5 seconds OR 12x per minute
-IF- 12 - 15 years  ventilate once every 3 - 4 seconds OR 14 - 26x per minute
-IF- 5 - 11 years  ventilate once every 2 - 3 seconds OR 16 - 34x per minute
ventilate once every 2 - 3 seconds OR 20 - 40x per minute
ventilate once every 2 seconds OR 25 - 55x per minute

title: ASTHMA - UNRESPONSIVE WITH PULSE
content: ventilate once every 8 - 12 seconds OR 5 - 8x per minute
-IF- 5 - 11 years  ventilate once every 5 - 6 seconds OR 10 - 14x per minute
-IF- 2 - 4 years  ventilate once every 4 - 5 seconds OR 12 - 15x per minute

title: DEFINITION
content: This guideline refers to any instance of NEW onset decreased responsiveness

title: RECOGNITION
content: Cause(s) of acute altered conscious state may not always be obvious – in such cases use the
following AEIOUTIPS pneumonic to consider some possible differentials
-E- Epilepsy E.G. Seizure activity OR postictal phase
Encephalitis (Hepatic) Think jaundice + liver failure / alcohol abuse
-U- Underdose E.G. Missed medications OR withdrawal
Trauma E.G. Head injury, exsanguination, etc.
Perfusion I.E. Marked hypotension and/or bradycardia
Psychiatric condition E.G. Panic attack, psychosis, etc.

title: STOP
content: Consider risk of needlestick injuries from used sharps whenever drug overdose is suspected
Consider risk of potential patient volatility when drug overdose is suspected
Consider that hypoxic patients may present with agitation until oxygen levels are corrected

title: ALL ALTERED CONSCIOUS PATIENTS – GENERAL MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Patient is biting DON’T insert anything past teeth
-IF- 12 - 15 years ventilate every 3 - 4 seconds OR 14 - 26x per minute
-IF- 5 - 11 years ventilate every 2 - 3 seconds OR 16 - 34x per minute
-IF- 1 - 4 years ventilate every 2 - 3 seconds OR 20 - 40x per minute
-IF- Patient is biting DON’T insert anything past teeth
Thorough VSS  Including SpO2, BGL, temperature, pupils, etc.
Consider possible causes (AEIOUTIPS) + manage appropriately where possible

title: HEAD INJURY – ADDITIONAL MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Life threatening bleeding   Control bleeding + dress wound
-ONLY IF- Necessary for ventilation  Airway adjunct

title: OVERDOSE (DRUGS / MEDICATIONS) – ADDITIONAL MANAGEMENT
content: Evidence suggestive of illicit drug use may include
Paraphernalia E.G. Used needles, empty packets, etc.
Where safe and possible to do so gain the following information
Route Method of consumption (E.G. oral, injected)
Agent Drug(s) and / or medication(s) consumed
Intent Intended self-harm OR accidental overdose
Emesis Have they vomited since consumption
Symptoms Any other symptoms since consumption

title: SEIZURE – ADDITIONAL MANAGEMENT
content: Do NOT forcibly restrict the patient’s body from convulsing
-IF- Available (E.G. prescribed medications) Assist carer(s) to administer seizure plan

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: A thorough and systematic primary should be performed on any altered conscious patient.
This includes any patient who deteriorates and becomes altered conscious while in Hatzolah
Consider that multiple agents (including alcohol) may be simultaneously involved in overdose
For the ingestion of any foreign agent, consider consulting poisons on 13 11 26

title: STOP
content: This guideline is NOT to be used on newborns
-IF- Unconscious -&- NO pulse Manage as Cardiac Arrest!
-IF- Unconscious -&- YES pulse Manage as per Box A
-IF- Conscious -&- Ineffective cough Manage as per Box B
-IF- Nil improved -&- Pulse present   Oxygen, BVM (8-15L O2), x2 Ventilations
-AS- Required -IF- Pulse present Repeat: 5x compressions + x2 ventilations
-IF- Paediatric Pt -&- Safe to do so Use gravity to assist with the follow manoeuvres
-IF- Still obstructed Chest thrusts, up to 5
-IF- Still obstructed Repeat back blows + chest thrusts, as above

title: ESCALATION OF CARE
content: -IF- No immediate improvement SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch + call ambulance

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Do NOT place fingers in patient’s mouth as this may trigger a bite reflex
If pulse is NOT found or is lost at any stage manage as per Cardiac Arrest!
If the patient recovers from basic first aid and ambulance is not attending, a minimum of a

title: DEFINITION
content: A disturbance in behaviour or mental function during or after alcohol consumption. Alcohol is
a depressant that affects the CNS. Intoxication may lead to inhibition of behaviours, slurred
speech, reduction in conscious state, HR and RR.

title: RECOGNITION
content: Patient is considered low risk (as per following criteria)
Has capacity to make their own decisions
Competent sober adult able to care for patient in a safe place

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Necessary Manage as per Altered Conscious State (Acute)
-IF- Necessary Manage as per Nausea / Vomiting
Advise not to drink any more alcohol until recovered from symptoms
Sleep on side, keep warm and remain in a low stimulus environment
Take paracetamol for mild pain as required
Have sober adult stay with the patient
Call for ambulance if conscious state deteriorates, patient vomits when laying supine and
does not clear their own airway, severe pain occurs, there is no improvement in
conscious state over the next 2 to 4 hours

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: This CPG is for 16 years and over
Patients who do not fit the low-risk criteria (i.e. moderate and high Risk) will be escalated

title: DEFINITION
content: For the purposes of this CPG a mild skin allergy is defined as allergic symptoms confined to the
skin only (single body system). If multiple body systems are being affected then treat as per

title: STOP
content: -IF- Meets RASH criteria                                       Mx as per Anaphylaxis / Call ambulance

title: MANAGEMENT
content: Monitor for 30/60 for deterioration or improvement
Leave in care of a responsible third party to observe with advice to seek help/call
ambulance if patient deteriorates (e.g. develops SOB, ALOC, GIT upset)
Advise patient to follow up with their GP regarding incident

title: DEFINITION
content: A severe, potentially life-threatening, systemic hypersensitivity reaction. Typically caused by
an excessive immune system response to an antigen that is normally harmless to humans.

title: RECOGNITION
content: -IF- Sudden onset of symptoms (usually within 30 minutes but may be up to 4 hours)
-&- Symptoms from 2 or more of the following R.A.S.H categories
-REGARDLESS- Whether or not there has been exposure to a known antigen
-OR- Isolated respiratory distress -&- Exposure to a KNOWN antigen
Insect stings: Bees, wasps, jumping jacks ants
Foods: Peanuts / tree nuts, fish / shellfish, dairy products, soy, sesame seeds, wheat
Medications: Antibiotics, anaesthetic drugs, contrast media

title: STOP
content: -IF- 12 - 15 years ventilate every 3 - 4 seconds OR 14 - 26x per minute
-IF- 5 - 11 years ventilate every 2 - 3 seconds OR 16 - 34x per minute
-IF- 1 - 4 years ventilate every 2 - 3 seconds OR 20 - 40x per minute

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -ELSE IF- Breathing difficulties Position: Sitting / tripoding
-IF- Safe / possible to do so Remove antigen (E.G. bee stinger)
-IF- Worsens OR no change @ 5 mins Consult clinician regarding need for repeat
-REGARDLESS- Of severity / improvement Transport to hospital

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Anaphylaxis can be difficult to identify, while rash / itch are common they are NOT mandatory
For the purposes of this guideline a ‘known antigen’ refers to any substance that has
previously caused an anaphylactic reaction in this patient
Adrenaline saves lives in anaphylaxis! NEVER delay adrenaline in favour of other medications!
Advise patient of possible adrenaline side effects including heart racing / palpitations / anxiety
Hospital based observations are required for a minimum of 4 hours

title: ANAPHYLAXIS, 48
content: Initial Dose 10 mg 2 ampules With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 5 mg @ 5 min 1 ampule With 8L O2
Initial Dose 5 mg 1 ampule With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 5 mg @ 20 min 1 ampule With 8L O2
Initial Dose 2.5 mg 1/2 ampule With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 2.5 mg @ 20 min 1/2 ampule With 8L O2

title: DEFINITION
content: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition characterized by acute episodes of airway swelling,
constriction and hyper mucus secretion. This can lead to wheezing, difficulty breathing,
chest tightness, coughing and – in extreme cases – respiratory arrest and death.
Asthma exacerbations may be triggered in susceptible individuals due to factors such as

title: STOP
content: Ventilate once every...    Oxygen, BVM (8-15L O2)

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Conscious Position: Sitting / Position of comfort
-IF- pMDI available -&- Mild / Moderate   Salbutamol, pMDI via Spacer
4 to 12 doses, 4 breaths for each dose
-IF- Mild -&- Responds to Mx Use VVED as minimum
-IF- No pMDI -OR- Severe    Salbutamol, Nebulised (8L O2)
-UNTIL- Difficult breathing relieved Repeat: 5 mg (1 neb) @ 5 mins
-IF- Severe resp. distress despite Salbutamol Consider if Anaphylaxis

title: ESCALATION OF CARE
content: -IF- Moderate, severe -OR- thunderstorm Call for Ambulance / 000

title: STOP
content: Ventilate once every...    Oxygen, BVM (8-15L O2)

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Conscious Position: Sitting / Position of comfort
-IF- pMDI available -&- Mild / Moderate   Salbutamol, pMDI via Spacer
4 to 12 doses, 4 breaths for each dose
-IF- Mild -&- Responds to Mx Use VVED as minimum
-IF- No pMDI -OR- Severe    Salbutamol, Nebulised (8L O2)
-IF- Severe resp. distress despite Salbutamol Consider if Anaphylaxis

title: ESCALATION OF CARE
content: -IF- Moderate, severe -OR- thunderstorm Call for Ambulance / 000

content: Ventilate once every...    Oxygen, BVM (8-15L O2)

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Conscious Position: Sitting / Position of comfort

4 to 12 doses, 4 breaths for each dose
-IF- Mild -&- Responds to Mx Use VVED as minimum
-IF- No pMDI -OR- Severe    Salbutamol, Nebulised (8L O2)
-IF- Severe resp. distress despite Salbutamol Consider if Anaphylaxis

title: ESCALATION OF CARE
content: -IF- Moderate, severe -OR- thunderstorm Call for Ambulance / 000

title: STOP
content: Ventilate once every...    Oxygen, BVM (8-15L O2)

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Conscious Position: Sitting / Position of comfort
-IF- pMDI available -&- Mild / Moderate   Salbutamol, pMDI via Spacer
2 to 6 doses, 4 breaths for each dose
-IF- Mild -&- Responds to Mx Use VVED as minimum
-IF- No pMDI -OR- Severe    Salbutamol, Nebulised (8L O2)
-IF- Severe resp. distress despite Salbutamol Consider if Anaphylaxis

title: ESCALATION OF CARE
content: -IF- Moderate, severe -OR- thunderstorm Call for Ambulance / 000

title: BIRTH & NEWBORN RESUSCITATION, 58
content: Initial Dose 4 – 12 puffs 4x breaths per puff Via spacer
Repeat Dose(s) 4 – 12 puffs @ 20 min 4x breaths per puff Via spacer
Initial Dose 2 – 6 puffs 4x breaths per puff Via spacer
Repeat Dose(s) 2 – 6 puffs @ 20 min 4x breaths per puff Via spacer

title: BIRTH & NEWBORN RESUSCITATION, 59
content: Initial Dose 10 mg 2 ampules With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 5 mg @ 5 min 1 ampule With 8L O2
Initial Dose 5 mg 1 ampule With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 5 mg @ 20 min 1 ampule With 8L O2
Initial Dose 2.5 mg 1/2 ampule With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 2.5 mg @ 20 min 1/2 ampule With 8L O2

title: BIRTH & NEWBORN RESUSCITATION, 60
content: Ipratropium Bromide  -IF- No improvement after 20 mins Salbutamol
Ipratropium Bromide  -IF- No improvement after 20 mins Salbutamol

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: If 000 had NOT been called OR ambulance is NOT attending for mild asthma the VVED must
Asthmatic patients can show initial improvement with treatment prior to rapid deterioration -
Improving SpO2 may NOT necessary be a sign of improvement in clinical condition
Beware of patient presenting with wheeze -&- history of heart failure but -NO- history of
asthma / COPD - they do NOT meet his guideline
Over ventilation of the asthmatic patient may result in worse patient outcomes
Management is primarily targeted at relieving difficult breathing - AVOID administering large
doses of Salbutamol when wheeze is the only ongoing concern
This guideline is only designed for patients that are 2 years old and over

title: STOP
content: For ALL birthing emergencies SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch + call ambulance
-IF- ANY of the following Consult: Clinician + PIPER
Presenting part of baby is anything other than their head (E.G. foot, buttocks, etc)
Mother has a very complicated medical history

title: RECOGNITION
content: Signs of imminent birth may include
Urination / defecation (from mother) or Urge to use bowels / bladder
Baby partly in view (E.G. head, foot, buttocks, etc)
Contractions becoming continuous / feeling like they’re running into each other
Stating: “The baby is coming!” / “I am going to have the baby!” (stated by mother)

title: PREPERATION
content: Clean + warm environment (consider turning heaters on)
Newborn BVM (connect tubing to oxygen tank but leave oxygen turned off to start with)
Suction (ensure working and within arm’s reach)

title: CURRENT PREGNANCY
content: Have you had any antenatal care? (E.G. midwife / doctor / ultrasound appointments etc.)
Number of weeks gestation? (37+ weeks considered full term, 22+ weeks considered viable)
Number of babies expected? (E.G. singleton, twins, triplets, etc)
Have your membranes ruptured? What time? What colour? What quantity? Any strange smell?
Do you know what position your baby is in? (E.G. cephalic, breached)
Has the baby been moving normally?
Any complications with the pregnancy (E.G. gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, etc)

title: PREVIOUS PREGNANCIES
content: Number of times you have been pregnant? Number of children?
Any complications with previous pregnancies or births? (E.G. c-section)

title: BIRTH: HEAD
content: Gently place fingers on baby’s head
-IF- Baby advancing slowly / steadily Advise mother: PUSH with contractions
-IF- Baby advancing extremely quickly Advise mother: PANT with contractions
-IF- Baby advancing extremely quickly Apply gentle downward pressure on baby
Do NOT hold baby back forcibly

title: UMBILICAL CORD CHECK
content: Do NOT cut cord routinely - ONLY cut cord if advised below
Assess: If cord wrapped around baby’s neck
-IF- Cord loose -&- Wrapped once Slip cord over babies head
-IF- Cord tight -OR- Multiple wraps Clamp and cut cord
Place 1st clamp 10cm from baby + 2nd clamp 15cm from baby (5cm from first clamp)

title: BIRTH: HEAD ROTATION
content: With next contraction the baby’s head will turn to face one of the mothers thighs (restitution)
This indicates internal rotation of shoulders in preparation for birth of body

title: BIRTH: SHOULDERS + BODY
content: May be passive or guided birth
Apply gentle downwards pressure to deliver top shoulder
Apply gentle upwards pressure to deliver bottom shoulder
-IF- Nil birth of body within 1 min of head Consult: Clinician

title: BIRTH & NEWBORN RESUSCITATION, 68
content: Stimulate by drying with towel (30 seconds)
-ONLY IF- Airway obstruction suspected Suction: Mouth then Nose
Auscultate: Heart Rate Normal is roughly 2 or more beats every 1 second

title: -IF- HR  100
content: Room Air, Newborn BVM  Ventilate once every...
Do NOT over inflate the lungs
And each time manage below as appropriate

title: MOTHER MANAGEMENT: PART 1
content: -IF- Major bleeding from visible lacerations Place pad + direct pressure

title: BIRTH: PLACENTA
content: Do NOT pull on umbilical cord!
-IF- Appropriate Encourage breast feeding (assists birth of placenta)
Placenta separates WITHOUT intervention May take 15-60 minutes - signs include:
Position: Mother squatting To allow for gravity to assist expulsion
Advise mother: “To give a little push” to help birth placenta
Use 2 hands to support + remove placenta using a twisting “see saw” motion
Ease membranes slowly out of the vagina
Place placenta + blood clots in container

title: MOTHER MANAGEMENT: PART 2
content: -IF- Major bleeding from visible lacerations Place pad + direct pressure

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Newborn refers to first few minutes of life to hours post birth
Stimulation can be applied by gentle rubbing the (very fragile) skin and tapping of the feet
Maintaining newborn warmth is absolutely essential – Do NOT base warming decisions on
whether or not you feel cold yourself
If suctioning then ALWAYS suction the Mouth before the Nose – You can remember this
important order as “M” comes before “N” alphabetically
Cord cutting is only mandatory to enable effective resuscitation – There is NO requirement to
cut the cord of the vigorous newborn

title: DEFINITION
content: Cardiac arrest refers to the hearts sudden inability to pump blood effectively around the body.
Without immediate intervention this will lead to death - typically within minutes.

title: RECOGNITION
content: The 3 signs that a patient is in cardiac arrest are no response, ineffective breathing + no pulse.
These should be discovered during the primary survey as follows

title: DEFIBRILLATOR + CPR
content: Ensure NO ONE touching patient prior / during shock (includes stopping compressions)

title: CARDIAC ARREST, 73
content: -OR- Single officer Ventilate via BVM 15L O2 @ 30 : 2 , consider LMA
-&- Multiple officers Ventilate via BVM 15L O2 @ 15 : 2, consider LMA
Repeat EVERY 2 minutes From top of this box (I.E. DEFIBRILLATOR + CPR)
-IF- Multiple officers Alternate: CPR responder 2 minutely

title: RETURN OF SPONTANEOUS CIRCULATION (ROSC)
content: -IF- 12 - 15 years ventilate every 3 - 4 seconds OR 14 - 26x per minute
-IF- 5 - 11 years ventilate every 2 - 3 seconds OR 16 - 34x per minute
-IF- 1 - 4 years ventilate every 2 - 3 seconds OR 20 - 40x per minute
-IF- Accredited to do so 12 Lead ECG

title: CARDIAC ARREST, 74
content: PAEDIATRIC CARDIAC ARREST RATES – 0 to 11 Years
Age Category Rescuers Rate Technique Ratio
DO pause for ventilations if NO LMA being used
Do NOT pause for ventilations if LMA is being used

title: CARDIAC ARREST, 75
content: Age Category Rescuers Rate Technique Ratio
DO pause for ventilations if NO LMA being used
Do NOT pause for ventilations if LMA is being used

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: In cases of Traumatic Cardiac Arrest, managing correctable causes is priority including
Haemorrhage control, Airway & Breathing Management, and Pelvic Splinting
Appropriate defibrillation and continuous CPR saves lives!
Keep all interruptions to compressions to an absolute minimum.
Hands should be positioned in the middle of the chest (lower half of the sternum) for effective
CPR depth should be 1/3 depth of the chest
Effective ventilation is guided by rise and fall of the chest with a minimum pause in CPR

title: DEFINITION
content: Cardiac chest pain specifically refers to chest pain that originates from the heart or its
surrounding structures. It is often associated with Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS). ACS refers
to a spectrum of conditions that are primarily associated with a sudden blockage of the
coronary arteries and subsequent reduction in blood flow to the heart muscle. This represents
a time critical and life-threatening complication.

title: RECOGNITION
content: Cardiac chest pain can be notoriously difficult to differentiate from non-cardiac chest pain -
when in doubt the “payoff principle” advised to treat as if it were cardiac
Chest pain associated with ANY of the following characteristics or symptoms should markedly
increase suspicion that it may be of a cardiac nature
History of experiencing similar pain during previous cardiac events
Pain that radiates to the shoulder, arm, jaw, neck, upper abdomen OR back
Pain that is worse on exertion (E.G. walking, climbing stairs)
Pain that is constant at rest (E.G. not significantly worse when coughing)
The following risk factors also increase the likelihood of chest pain having a cardiac origin
Family / genetic history of cardiac events

title: MANAGEMENT
content: SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch + call ambulance Treat as time critical
-IF- Suspecting Cardiac Chest Pain   Aspirin, Oral
-IF- No side effects Maximum: No maximum - within reason
Reassess: Side effects after each dose
-OR- Side effects Remove tablet + rinse mouth
-IF- No side effects Maximum: 6 mL (2 vials) in 24 hour period

title: CARDIAC CHEST PAIN / DISCOMFORT, 79
content: Glyceryl Trinitrate GTN -IF- Cardiac Chest Pain / Discomfort
Repeat Dose(s) 3 mL PRN 3 mL typically lasts for 25 minutes continuous use

title: DEFINITION
content: Dehydration refers to a detrimental reduction of the amount of water in the body

title: RECOGNITION
content: Less than adequate perfusion as per the Perfusion Status Assessment.
The patient is considered to have less than adequate ( Adequate) perfusion -IF- 2 or more
of the below criteria are outside of the Adequate criteria

title:  50 -OR-  100
content: No Perfusion Cool, Pale, Clammy No pulse Unable to record Unconscious
Pulse and sBP in this table specifically refer to  15 year olds only
Poor fluid Intake of fluid vs fluid loss

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Able to tolerate oral fluids Encourage patient to drink water
Gain IV access  Normal Saline, IV
IV access and IV therapy may only be performed by accredited responders!
Administer lower doses to patients that are elderly OR have renal OR heart failure
Initial Dose 500 mL Titrate to response
-&- NOT elderly / NO renal OR heart failure
Initial Dose 1000 mL Titrate to response

title: RECOGNITION
content: This guideline is intended for any ADULT patient that has a fall of  than 1 meter -&- is alert

title: ASSESSMENT
content: Did anyone witness / overhear the fall?
Does the patient recall the fall?
Why did the patient fall? E.G. Trip, fainted, chronic weakness, unknown
Were there symptoms prior to falling? E.G. Chest pain, stroke, breathing difficulties
Position they fell from? E.G. Standing, seated, leaning against a wall
Any attempt to control the fall? E.G. Outstretched hands, lowered down by family
What surface did they fall onto? E.G. Carpet, tiles, concrete, bed
Objects struck during fall? E.G. Chest strike on corner of coffee table
Was there a head strike? If yes – against what?
Was there a loss of consciousness? If so – how long for?
-IF- Accredited to do so 12 Lead ECG
-IF- Potential head strike Assess: Head injury
-IF- ANY of the following signs Hospital transport

title: FALLS, 83
content: Bruising to head behind the ear
-IF- ANY of the following signs Virtual ED
-IF- Required (Refer to spinal trauma) Spinal immobilisation
-IF- ANY signs of spinal injury Hospital transport
Sensory deficits: General Ask: Any numb, tingle, burn OR altered sensation
Sensory deficits: Arms Assess: Light touch across palm & back of hand
Sensory deficits: Legs Assess: Light touch lateral side of calcaneus
Motor deficits: Arms Assess: Ability to grasp, pull & push
Motor deficits: Legs Assess: Ability to plantar flex, dorsiflex & leg raise
Midline tenderness on palpation Assess: Full length of spine
Can’t rotate neck 45° left & right E.G. Causes pain
History of spinal abnormality E.G. Disease, surgery, known weakness OR injury
-IF- ANY of the following signs Hospital transport
Unequal rise and fall of chest

title: FALLS, 84
content: -IF- ANY of the following signs Hospital transport
-IF- ANY of the following signs Hospital transport
-IF- ANY injury requiring wound care Hospital OR Private transport to PPCC
-IF- ANY signs of significant injury Hospital transport
-IF- ANY of the following signs Hospital transport

title: FALLS, 85
content: -IF- ANY of the following signs Virtual ED

title: GCS  15
content: -REGARDLESS- whether or not normal for patient
Assess: Confirm cause of fall (AEIOU TIPS)
-IF- Fall was caused by syncope / faint Assess: High risk syncope / faint
-IF- ANY high risk syncope symptoms Hospital transport

title: FALLS, 86
content: -IF- ANY other syncope / faint Virtual ED + forward 12 Lead ECG (if available)
-IF- Pt likely to re-fall within 24 hours Hospital transport
Postural changes E.G. Dizziness / difficulty breathing on standing
Assess: Ability to meet care needs
-IF- Unable to be cared for at home Hospital transport
-IF- ANY high risk factors Hospital transport
No / poor recollection of event -UNLESS- Normal for patient (E.G. dementia)
Blood in stools OR blood in vomit

title: FALLS, 87
content: -IF- ANY moderate risk factors Virtual ED
Poor recollection -BUT- Normal for patient (E.G. dementia)
Anticoagulants E.G. Apixaban, dabigatran, rivaroxaban, warfarin
Antiplatelets (other than aspirin) E.G. Clopidogrel, ticagrelor, prasugrel
Refer patient to Hatzolah social worker

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Recommend hospital transport -IF- patient meets ANY “Hospital Transport” criteria
Recommend Virtual ED -IF- patient meets ANY “Virtual ED” criteria -&- NO “Hospital Transport”
Call Ambulance Victoria if hospital transport is required
An “uncontrolled fall” is one where the patients decent was NOT slowed (E.G. by being caught
by a bystander who then gently lowered them to the floor)
Hatzolah members are advised to escalate care if they have any further concerns
A patient is considered intoxicated when they are inebriated to a point where they are unable
to be accurate historians – in most cases this does not refer to the patient who has had a glass
All patients who are not attended to by ambulance or VVED, must be advised to see their GP to
discuss the fall and have a medical review. Ensure the patient can mobilise as per their normal,
is left with another person, has access to a phone, has access to the bathroom, food, water, and
advised to call for help should they deteriorate or become concerned.

title: DEFINITION
content: Hypoglycaemia is a condition characterised by low blood sugar levels

title: RECOGNITION
content: Use this guideline -IF- Blood Glucose Level (BGL)  4 mmol/L

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Responding -&- Can swallow   Glucose Paste, Oral
Never administer oral medications to patients that are in an altered conscious state!
-IF- NOT responding -OR- Can’t swallow   Glucagon, IM
Only accredited responders may administer Glucagon!
-ONCE- Can swallow safely Advise: Eat long lasting carbohydrates
Within 20 minutes – E.G. Sandwich, fruit, glass of milk

title: REFER TO GP
content: Refer patient to GP if -ALL- of the following criteria are met
GCS has fully returned to the patients normal baseline
Patient has a known pre-existing diagnosis of diabetes
Only oral glucose was required to correct BGLs
There was a known cause of this episode of hypoglycaemia (e.g. forgot to eat dinner)
No injury or seizure has occurred
Patient willing and able to eat long lasting carbohydrates
There are NONE of the following risks for prolonged or recurrent hypoglycaemia:
Overdose on medication (either accidental or intentional)
Suspected cause of hypoglycaemia requires further investigation (e.g. infection)
The patient is able to be monitored by a responsible adult for at least 4 hours

title: -IF- BGL  4
content: Demographic All ages -&- Responding -&- Can swallow safely

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Severe hypoglycaemia may cause some patients to act aggressively, consider scene safety!
BGL should be measured in patients with any of the following
The patient may become hypoglycaemic once more if the underlying cause is not addressed

title: DEFINITION
content: Infection refers to the body becoming invaded by harmful microorganisms. These
microorganisms multiply, cause damage to the body tissue and trigger an immune response
which itself may become detrimental to the body.
Sepsis is a life threatening condition that occurs when the body’s own response to an infection
Injures its own tissues and organs. Sepsis can lead to shock, multiple organ failure and death.
In some circumstances – if managed incorrectly – infections may spread between people.
For this reason certain precautions and Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) must be
maintained by Hatzolah staff to ensure patient and staff safety.

title: GENERAL SYMPTOMS OF INFECTION
content: Malaise I.E. General feeling of being unwell

title: COMMON SOURCES OF INFECTIONS
content: S Surgery / Hospital acquired E.G. Recent surgery / stay in hospital
U Urinary E.G. Painful / discoloured / malodorous urination
N Neurological E.G. Headache, neck stiffness, photophobia
W Wounds E.G. Lacerations, pressure ulcers, cellulitis
A Abdominal (I.E. Gastrointestinal) E.G. Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdo. cramping
R Respiratory E.G. SOB, coughing, sneezing, sore throat, rhinitis
D Device  E.G. Pacemakers, insulin pumps, urinary catheters

title: SYSTEMIC INFLAMMATORY RESPONSE SYNDROME CRITERIA / SIRS CRITERIA
content: A patient meets the SIRS criteria if you suspect they may have an infection
-&- They have 2 or more of the following abnormal vital signs:

title: STOP
content: -IF- Meeting Red Flag vital signs Call for Ambulance / 000

title: MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Meeting SIRS criteria Use VVED as minimum
-IF- None of the above Refer to GP as minimum

title: STANDARD PRECAUTIONS
content: Must be used with all patients and includes:
Respiratory hygiene (I.E. covering mouth when sneezing and cleaning hands afterwards)
Safe use and disposal of sharps (E.G. needles)
Appropriate cleaning of reusable equipment between patients
Routine environmental cleaning (E.G. cleaning / disinfecting stretchers, etc)

title: CONTACT PRECAUTIONS
content: Must be used in addition to standard precautions whenever there is a risk of direct or indirect
contact transmission of pathogenic microorganisms (E.G. possible contact with body fluid OR
patients with known / possible contact transmissible disease)
Gown / apron – if splash is likely
Eye protection (I.E. safety goggles) – if splash is likely
Fluid resistant mask – if splash is likely
Appropriate distancing to avoid splash contamination
Ensure infected areas of the patient’s body are appropriately contained and covered
Communication of patients infectious status to other health providers

title: DROPLET & AIRBORNE PRECATUIONS
content: Must be used in addition to standard precautions with all patients meeting ANY of the
Known / suspected to have a droplet OR airborne transmissible disease regardless of
their current symptoms (as such diseases can be spread simply by talking or breathing)
Patient must wear a mask covering both their mouth and nose – and leave it on
Patient to practice respiratory hygiene (I.E. covering mouth when sneezing and cleaning
Appropriate distancing from medical providers whenever possible
Extra cleaning of equipment and environment after patient use

title: RECOGNITION
content: Use this guideline for patients meeting either of the following criteria
Nausea / vomiting that is NOT being tolerated

title: STOP
content: -IF- Altered conscious  Position: Recovery position / laterally

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Maintain spinal immobilisation when placing potential spinal patients in lateral position
ALL patients receiving ondansetron must have a VVED consult attended as a minimum if not
being transported by ambulance to hospital.

title: RECOGNITION
content: O Onset time of the pain?
O Other symptoms associated with the pain?
R Relief from the pain? (E.G. Positional relief, home medications already tried)

title: PAIN RELIEF (NON-CARDIAC), 99
content: Confirm the following doses with the label on the bottle prior to administration!
Repeat Dose(s) -IF- Required 15 mg x kg @ 4 hours

title: PAIN RELIEF (NON-CARDIAC), 100
content: Repeat Dose(s) 3 mL PRN 3 mL typically lasts for 25 minutes continuous use

title: DEFINITION
content: Sudden, uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain which can cause changes in behaviour,

title: RECOGNITION
content: Seizures can present due to a wide variety of causes and with a wide variety of symptoms.
Differentiating between types and causes of seizures in the pre-hospital environment can be
difficult. When in doubt it is prudent to err on the side of caution and treat likely seizure
HOWEVER Midazolam may ONLY be administered by accredited responders when one of
the two following criteria are met:
Midazolam is indicated for patients with Generalised Convulsive Status Epilepticus (GCSE)
characterised by ongoing generalized tonic-clonic movements -&- altered conscious state
-OR- presented as multiple seizures without full recovery of conscious state in between

title: STOP
content: -ONLY IF- Adequate perfusion Titrate down to SpO2 92 - 96%

title: SEIZURE
content: -IF- Risk of injury Pillow / pad head
Do NOT forcibly restrict the patient’s body from convulsing
-IF- Available (E.G. prescribed medications) Assist carer(s) to administer seizure plan
Only accredited responders may administer Midazolam!

title: SEIZURE, 103
content: Multiple GCSE refers to multiple tonic clonic seizures occurring WITHOUT full recovery to the
Patients normal baseline in between seizures

title: DEFINITION
content: A stroke occurs when there is either a blockage to the blood supply of part of the brain OR
when a blood vessel in the brain bursts. In either case brain cells are deprived of oxygen and
nutrients rapidly leading to potentially irreversible brain damage or death.

title: RECOGNITION
content: An acute stroke should be suspected when ANY of the following signs and symptoms occur
-F- Facial droop  E.G. One side of the face doesn’t move as well as the other
Assess by asking patient to smile or show their teeth
-A- Arm / Limb weakness E.G. One sided limb weakness OR arm drift
Assess by asking patient to hold their arms straight for 10s & squeeze your fingers
-S- Speech deficit  E.G. Slurs words, says incorrect words OR is unable to speak
Assess by asking patient to repeat “you can’t teach an old dog new tricks”
-&- These signs and symptoms have existed for  24 hours
-&- Blood Glucose Level (BGL) is normal (I.E.  4 mmol/L)

title: STROKE HISTORY
content: What time were these stroke symptoms first noticed? (As precisely as possible)
When was the patient last known / seen to be well? (I.E. stroke symptom free)
Have any of these symptoms occurred previously in the past? If yes - What caused them?
Have they had a stroke before?
Are they currently drug or alcohol affected?

title: STOP
content: -IF- Unconscious Manage per unconscious patient guideline

title: -IF- BGL  4
content: Manage hypoglycaemia then revaluate for stroke
-REGARDLESS- Of severity / improvement SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch + call ambulance

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: The following symptoms may be caused by strokes and should therefore prompt you to
In cases where a patient wakes up with stroke symptoms the time of onset is taken from when
the patient was last seen well and NOT from the time of awakening
Stroke symptoms can sometimes fluctuate / be intermittent – for this reason all potential
stroke patients should be transported to hospital regardless of symptom improvement OR

title: DEFINITION
content: Trauma refers to bodily injury caused by a sudden application of significant force (E.G. Fall).
Such force may be blunt (E.G. Head strike against concrete) OR penetrating (E.G. Punctured by
a knife). In either case it results in damage to tissue and organs, ranging from minor cuts to
Trauma may also be localised and occur to a specific body region (E.G. Heavy object falling on a
foot) OR it may be undifferentiated (E.G. Car crash, falls, etc). Undifferentiated trauma requires
a systematic head to toe assessment to ensure no injuries are missed.

title: PRINCIPLES OF MAJOR TRAUMA
content: In cases of Traumatic Cardiac Arrest, managing correctable causes is priority including
Haemorrhage control, Airway & Breathing Management, and Pelvic Splinting
Maintain the patients head in neutral position avoiding neck flexion/extension
ONLY utilise airway adjuncts (OP, NP) if airway not patent and ventilation required
Hypothermia is a significant concern leading to increased mortality.
Warming the patient is important and where appropriate should NOT be delayed!
The Mechanism of injury is a significant risk factor. Thorough History taking is important to
identify such mechanisms and subsequent care/priority.

title: MANAGEMENT – MAJOR HAEMORHAGE CONTROL
content: -IF- Life threatening bleed Apply pad and pressure
-IF- Uncontrollable life threatening bleed Combat Application Tourniquet
-IF- Uncontrollable life threatening wound Quickclot trauma pad
-IF- Uncontrollable life threatening wound Quickclot rolled gauze
-IF- Obvious significant pelvic fracture Pelvic splint

title: MANAGEMENT – AIRWAY MANAGEMENT
content: AVOID causing patient to gag or vomit
-ONLY IF- Airway is NOT patent NPA
-ONLY IF- NPA attempt is UNSUCCESSFUL  OPA

title: MANAGEMENT – BREATHING MANAGEMENT
content: -ONLY IF- Adequate perfusion Titrate down to SpO2 92 - 96%

title: MANAGEMENT – HEAD TO TOE ASSESSMENT
content: -IF- Required Manage as per Head Injury guideline
-IF- Required Manage as per Spinal Injury guideline
-IF- Required Manage as per Chest Injury guideline

title: MANAGEMENT – HEAD INJURY
content: -IF- Meeting ANY of the following criteria Treat as time critical!

title: GCS  13
content: Cerebral Spinal Fluid (CSF) leaking from the ears OR nose
Battle sign (bruising on the skull behind the earlobes)
Other obvious sign of skull fracture
-IF- Meeting ANY of the following criteria Transport to hospital
-IF- Meeting ANY of the following criteria Virtual ED

title: MANAGEMENT – SPINAL INJURY
content: -IF- Meeting time critical criteria Treat as time critical!
Major trauma following blunt trauma to the head or trunk
ANY new motor deficits / weakness when asked to...
Legs: Push / plantar flex, pull / dorsiflex, leg raise
ANY new sensory deficits / reduced sensation when applying light touch to the following
Arms: Light touch across the palm and back of hand
Legs: Light touch to lateral side of calcaneus
The patient should be questioned regarding numbness, tingling, burning or any
other altered sensation, anywhere in the body
NOTE: The left and right sides should be tested simultaneously to compare strength
between each side of the body
Inability to rotate neck 45° left and right
Do NOT turn the patient’s head for them
Ask the patient to turn their head slowly left and right,
Advised them to stop if they feel ANY pain OR resistance

title: SPINAL TRAUMA, 111
content: Consider: Virtual ED -IF- age is the ONLY concerning feature post injury (E.G. fall)
-IF- ANY spinal immobilization criteria met Apply cervical collar
-IF- Patient meeting ALL following criteria Consider self-extrication
-IF- ANY spinal immobilization criteria met Spinal immobilisation
NEVER independently restrain the patients head to the stretcher
-IF- ANY spinal immobilization criteria met   Ondansetron, ODT

title: MANAGEMENT – CHEST INJURY
content: -IF- Spinal precaution -OR- Poor perfusion Position: Supine with slight head elevation
-IF- Awake / spontaneously ventilating Position: Sitting upright
-UNLESS- Life threatening bleed Do NOT cover open chest wounds

title: DEFINITION
content: A burn refers to damage to skin or other tissue primarily due to
Electrical injury E.G. lighting strikes, electrical grid injury
Chemical injury E.G. acids, industrial chemicals

title: STOP
content: Safety: Caution NOT to inhale smoke / substances present at scene!
Safety: Beware of injury from burnt clothing / chemical contamination!
Safety: Oxygen is flammable! Do NOT use oxygen near flames / sources of ignition!

title: RECOGNITION
content: Suspect airway burns -IF- any of the following
Burns to upper torso, neck or face
Incident occurred in an enclosed space
Singed facial hair (E.G. eyebrows / lashes, beard)
Any airway burns MUST be treated as Time Critical, SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch + call ambulance

title: MANAGEMENT
content: SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch Call ambulance + confirm airway burns
-IF- Partial thickness -OR- Full thickness SitRep: Hatzolah dispatch + Call ambulance
-IF- Burn location allows (E.G. hand)  Position: Elevate burn
Cool burn with CLEAN running water
For 20 minutes Including any cooling prior to Hatzolah arrival
Do NOT use ice water / dam water
Do NOT continue cooling after 20 mins
-AS- Required   Analgesia – As per Pain Relief (Non Cardiac)
-UNLESS- Stuck to skin Remove jewellery / clothing from burn
Apply loosely to allow for swelling
-IF- Conscious + alert -&- Delay to hospital Encourage patient to maintain hydration

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: If running water is not available, cooling may be achieved by immersing the injury in CLEAN
still water, using a spray bottle or applying moist towels
AVOID dirt water (E.G. dam water) as using it increases risk of infection
Aim to remove jewellery prior to swelling occurring (E.G. rings may become stuck)
Advise all patients not being attended by Ambulance Victoria or VVED with minor superficial

title: DEFINITION
content: A wound refers to a break in the continuity of the body
Wounds may be closed or open, where the skin has been broken
An open wound exposes the patient to risk of contamination and infection – This guideline
is intended for the management of open wounds

title: STOP
content: Safety: Ensure PPE is worn and perform 5 moments of hand hygiene
For uncontrolled haemorrhage manage as per General Trauma Approach

title: RECOGNITION
content: Suspect infection -IF- ANY of the following

title: ABRAISION - MANAGEMENT
content: Leave open air unless actively bleeding OR deep
Refer to GP for review within 24 hours

title: MAMAL BITES (INCLUDING HUMAN BITES) - MANAGEMENT
content: Leave open air unless actively bleeding
Refer to ED / VVED / PPCC within 2 hours
Antibiotics + Tetanus injections may be required

title: AVULSION - MANAGEMENT
content: Refer to ED / VVED for further review

title: CUT / LACERATION (MINOR) - MANAGEMENT
content: Cover with low-absorbent dressing to prevent further trauma and absorb exudate
Refer to GP for review within 24 hours

title: INCISION - MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Contaminated gently clean with 0.9% sodium chloride
Cover with NAD plus dressing OR island dressing
-IF- Closure is required refer to GP / PPCC / ED for review within 8 hours

title: PUNCTURE - MANAGEMENT
content: -IF- Soiled gently clean with 0.9% sodium chloride
Leave open OR apply dry dressing
Refer to GP for review within 4 hours

title: SKIN TEAR - MANAGEMENT
content: Carefully realign the skin flap (where possible) close to its original position
Use supplied tweezers and / or gauze moistened with saline
Apply Melolin plus bandage    silicone dressing
Refer to GP / PPCC / ED for review within 12 hours

title: TETANUS PRONE WOUNDS - MANAGEMENT
content: ANY wound other than a clean, minor cut should be treated as a tetanus prone wound
Tetanus injection is required within 24 hours

title: REFER TO VVED / ED
content: Refer to VVED / ED if ANY of the following
Wound to head, neck, face, hands OR genitals
Injuries occurred while submerged or in marine environment
Pain is unable to be controlled

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: Wound healing is optimised when the wound is cleansed to remove debris and devitalised
tissue, kept moist, infection is prevented, and the wound has adequate blood supply.
Primary closure: For a clean wound that requires closure (sutures/glue) review within 8 hours
Wounds to the face may cause unwanted scarring - for cosmetic reasons refer to definitive care
Fresh wound: Up to 8 hours since injury
Old wound: More than 8 hours since injury
Superficial: Only the epidermis and upper dermis is involved
Partial thickness: Skin loss has occurred down to the dermis
Full thickness: Skin and subcutaneous tissue is involved
Deep / complicated: Penetration has occurred into the natural cavities, an organ or tissue
Incision: Clean-edged wound from a sharp item e.g. a knife
Laceration: Commonly jagged-edged wound caused by shearing forces such as impact
Avulsion: Tearing away of skin from surfaces below
Abrasion: Surface layer of skin has been rubbed off by friction
Puncture: A narrow aperture wound which extends some distance into the tissues

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Hypovolaemic shock without adequate fluid replacement

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Do NOT delay immediate adrenaline administration in patients experiencing anaphylaxis
Consider consulting for reduced doses for
- Monoamine Oxidase Inhibitors (MAOIs) – Current / recent use
Consider consulting for increased doses for

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Chest pain associated with psychostimulant overdose where sBP is  160 mmHg
Actively bleeding peptic ulcers (E.G. blood in stool)

title: INDICATIONS
content: Mild allergy associated with skin symptoms only (E.G. Hives, welt, itchiness etc)

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Known hypersensitivity to cetirizine or hydroxyzine

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Avoid using alongside alcohol or other CNS depressants as this may cause sedation

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: MUST be accredited to administer IM Glucagon

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: MUST be able to swallow safely do NOT administer if altered conscious OR at reasonable

title: -IF- BGL  4
content: Demographic All ages -&- Responding -&- Can swallow safely

title: HR  50 BPM
content: Perform ECG prior to administration when accredited to do so

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Right ventricular myocardial infarction OR inferior myocardial infarction with sBP  160 mmHg
The following demographics are at higher risk of adverse effects
- No previous history of Glyceryl Trinitrate use
Preterm labour – Concurrent use with other toctolytics

title: PREGNANCY / POSTPARTUM
content: Use lowest effective dose and observe infant for adverse effects such as flushing or discomfort
Glyceryl Trinitrate GTN -IF- Cardiac Chest Pain / Discomfort

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Atropine hypersensitivity (including hypersensitivity to Atropines derivatives)

title: ADVERSE EFFECTS
content: Acute angle closure glaucoma secondary to direct eye contact (rare)

title: IPRATROPOUM BROMIDE, 134
content: Ipratropium Bromide  -IF- No improvement after 20 mins Salbutamol
Ipratropium Bromide  -IF- No improvement after 20 mins Salbutamol

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Malignant hyperthermia (know history or family history of)

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Patients should not be administered  6 mL of methoxyflurane in a 24 hour period,
due to increased risk of kidney damage
- Do NOT administer in a confined space
- Place used Penthrox inhalers in closed bag when not in use

title: METHOXYFLURANE, 136
content: Repeat Dose(s) 3 mL PRN 3 mL typically lasts for 25 minutes continuous use

title: INDICATIONS
content: Ongoing tonic clonic activity associated with altered consciousness AND lasting 5 or more
Multiple ongoing episodes of tonic clonic activity without full recovery of consciousness

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Midazolam may ONLY be administered by accredited responders

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Reduce doses for patients that are:
- Have chronic renal failure, CCF or shock
The CNS depressant effects of benzodiazepines are enhanced in the presence of narcotics
Can cause severe respiratory depression in patients with COPD

title: MIDAZOLAM, 138
content: Multiple GCSE refers to multiple tonic clonic seizures occurring WITHOUT full recovery to the
Patients normal baseline in between seizures

title: INDICATIONS
content: Fluid replacement for volume depleted patients in the context of dehydration
Fluid for the dilution or preparation of other IV medications if required

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Bilateral fine crackles on chest auscultation suggestive of APO

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Reduce doses for patients that are:

title: ADVERSE EFFECTS
content: Potential for fluid overload with high risk patients or large volumes

title: NORMAL SALINE, 140
content: Initial Dose 500 mL Titrate to response
-&- NOT elderly / NO renal OR heart failure
Initial Dose 1000 mL Titrate to response

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Apomorphine (medication typically used to treat Parkinsons disease)

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: First trimester pregnancy (0 to 12 weeks) without first consulting with receiving hospital
Severe liver disease (E.G. cirrhosis) – Do not exceed 8 mg total dose per day

title: ADVERSE EFFECTS
content: Visual disturbances (rarely associated with transient loss of vision)

title: PREGNANCY / POSTPARTUM

title: DEFINITION
content: Hypoxia occurs when there is inadequate oxygen in the tissues to maintain homeostasis. It
may lead to symptoms such as an altered conscious state, dyspnea, tachycardia, tachypnea,

title: FURTHER NOTES
content: This CPG is intended for patients aged 16 and older
High concentration oxygen may be harmful for patients at risk of hypercapnic respiratory
Past history of smoking (including current + ex-smokers)

title: INDICATIONS
content: Apply oxygen to all patients presenting with severe shortness of breath
-IF- ANY of the bellow conditions Oxygen: titrate SpO2 88 - 92%

title: COPD
content: -IF- ANY of the bellow conditions Oxygen: Initial Mx NRB 10-15 L/min
-ONCE- Hemodynamically stable Titrate oxygen to SpO2 92 - 96%
-IF- ANY of the bellow conditions Oxygen: ALWAYS Administer NRB 10-15 L/min

title: INDICATIONS
content: Moderate / severe pain when used in combination with other analgesics

title: CONTRINDICATIONS
content: Any form of paracetamol in children  1 month old
Paracetamol tablets in children  7 years old

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Liver toxicity may occur in paracetamol overdose
- Do NOT administer if paracetamol has already been given within past 4 hours
- Do NOT administer if total paracetamol within 24 hours exceeds 4 G in adults
- Do NOT administer if total paracetamol within 24 hours exceeds 60 mg/kg in children
Risk of liver toxicity increases in the following circumstances

title: ADVERSE EFFECTS
content: Hypotension (associated with IV infusion, particularly in critically ill patients)

title: PARACETAMOL, 147
content: Confirm the following doses with the label on the bottle prior to administration!
Repeat Dose(s) -IF- Required 15 mg x kg @ 4 hours

title: PRECAUTIONS
content: Large doses may cause intracellular metabolic acidosis

title: ADVERSE EFFECTS
content: Initial Dose 4 – 12 puffs 4x breaths per puff Via spacer
Repeat Dose(s) 4 – 12 puffs @ 20 min 4x breaths per puff Via spacer
Initial Dose 2 – 6 puffs 4x breaths per puff Via spacer
Repeat Dose(s) 2 – 6 puffs @ 20 min 4x breaths per puff Via spacer

title: DEVELOPMENT NOTES, 149
content: Initial Dose 10 mg 2 ampules With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 5 mg @ 5 min 1 ampule With 8L O2
Initial Dose 5 mg 1 ampule With 8L O2
Repeat Dose(s) 5 mg @ 20 min 1 ampule With 8L O2
